question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a denial-of-service against the stream file ingestion pipeline by repeatedly injecting large collections of signatures with totalStake=0 as the first element, causing getStakeRequiredForConsensus() to throw SignatureVerificationException on every call, preventing legitimate stream files from being processed and creating gaps in the mirror node's transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not check whether the node referenced in each signature is a legitimate network node (no cross-reference against an authoritative node list), injecting signatures from fabricated nodes with high stake values to boost a minority file hash group's stake above the consensus threshold, causing transaction history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not enforce that the sum of all individual node stakes equals totalStake, injecting signatures where each node reports a stake equal to totalStake (rather than a fraction of it), causing the stake sum for a minority hash group to be a multiple of totalStake and trivially meeting the 1/3 threshold, enabling history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method processes all hash groups in the signatureHashMap regardless of whether a consensus has already been found (no early exit inside the for loop), causing the CONSENSUS_REACHED status to be set on signatures from multiple conflicting hash groups if multiple groups independently meet the threshold, creating an inconsistent transaction history state in the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method uses the first signature's node.getTotalStake() as the authoritative total stake for the entire validation, while subsequent signatures may report different totalStake values, by injecting a first signature with a very high totalStake (making the threshold high) to prevent the legitimate hash group from reaching consensus while a second call with a low totalStake first-signature accepts a minority hash?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node to accept a stream file with a fraudulent transaction history by exploiting the fact that the validate() method does not verify that the fileHash in each signature corresponds to the actual content of the downloaded stream file, only that enough nodes signed the same hash value, allowing an attacker who can serve a modified stream file to nodes to get those nodes to sign a hash of the modified file?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method's signatureHashMap uses HashMultimap (which allows duplicate values per key) to inject the same StreamFileSignature object multiple times for the same file hash key, causing the stake for that hash group to be counted multiple times and artificially inflating it above the consensus threshold for a minority file hash?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not validate the SignatureType of each signature (only checks SignatureStatus == VERIFIED), injecting signatures with an unsupported or weak SignatureType that was incorrectly marked VERIFIED by a buggy upstream verifier, to boost a minority file hash group's stake above the consensus threshold and cause history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not check the StreamType of each signature, injecting signatures from a different stream type (e.g., balance stream signatures) into a record stream validation call, causing cross-stream signature mixing that inflates the stake for a minority file hash and enables transaction history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not enforce a minimum collection size before processing, passing a collection with exactly one VERIFIED signature whose node.getStake() equals or exceeds stakeRequiredForConsensus (possible when that node holds >= 1/3 of total stake), causing a single-node consensus that accepts a minority file hash and reorganizes transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method's stakeRequiredForConsensus is computed once and reused for all hash groups, by crafting a scenario where the totalStake from the first signature is set to a value that makes the threshold exactly equal to the stake of a minority hash group but not the majority group, causing the minority group to pass canReachConsensus() while the majority fails?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not verify that the filename stored in each signature matches the expected stream file being validated, injecting signatures for a different (attacker-controlled) filename that happen to have VERIFIED status and sufficient stake, causing the mirror node to accept a consensus for the wrong file and reorganize transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not check for empty or zero-length fileHash byte arrays in getFileHashAsHex(), causing DomainUtils.bytesToHex() to return an empty string as the hash key, which groups all signatures with empty fileHash into the same bucket regardless of their actual content, potentially allowing a minority group to reach consensus by merging with other empty-hash signatures?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method's loop sets stakeRequiredForConsensus only when filename == null (i.e., only for the first signature), meaning if the first signature has status != VERIFIED and is skipped in the signatureHashMap but still processed in the first-signature initialization block, the totalStake could be set from a non-VERIFIED signature with a manipulated totalStake value?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not validate that node.getStake() <= node.getTotalStake() for each signature, injecting signatures where a single node's stake exceeds the totalStake, causing the stake sum for a minority hash group to exceed totalStake and trivially pass canReachConsensus(), enabling transaction history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not enforce that the signatures collection contains signatures from a minimum number of distinct nodes, allowing a single node with >= 1/3 of total stake to unilaterally determine consensus for any file hash it signs, enabling a compromised or colluding node to reorganize transaction history without needing cooperation from other nodes?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method's signatureHashMap is a local variable (not persisted across calls), meaning each call to validate() is stateless and does not remember previously rejected hash groups, allowing an attacker to repeatedly call validate() with slightly different signature sets until a minority hash group accumulates enough stake to pass consensus in a single call?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not verify the temporal validity of signatures (no timestamp check), injecting old or replayed signatures from a previous consensus round that happen to have VERIFIED status and match a minority file hash for the current round, causing the mirror node to accept a reorged transaction history based on stale signatures?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not check whether the same node appears multiple times in the signatures collection with different fileHash values, allowing a single node's stake to be counted toward multiple hash groups simultaneously, potentially enabling a minority hash group to reach consensus by double-counting a high-stake node's contribution?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method's consensus check (consensusCount > 0) does not verify that the winning hash group represents the majority of stake (only >= 1/3), allowing a minority hash group with exactly 1/3 of stake to win over a majority group with 2/3 of stake if the majority group's signatures are suppressed, causing the mirror node to record a minority-consensus transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not validate the bytes field of each StreamFileSignature (the raw signature bytes), only checking the pre-computed status field, allowing an attacker who can set status=VERIFIED on a signature with arbitrary bytes to inject fake consensus for a minority file hash and reorganize transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not enforce that the fileHashSignature bytes in each StreamFileSignature are cryptographically valid for the corresponding fileHash, only relying on the upstream VERIFIED status, allowing a compromised upstream verifier to mark invalid signatures as VERIFIED and inject them into the consensus calculation for a minority file hash?
